metadata.title: Clifti-GPT benchmark AnnData: reference and query splits for federated single-cell experiments
metadata.description:
Clifti-GPT benchmark AnnData

Prepared reference and query AnnData (.h5ad) files used to reproduce Clifti-GPT experiments across six public single-cell cohorts. Each archive contains the two files required by the experiment registry in the Clifti-GPT repository. Gene expression is filtered to the scGPT vocabulary; highly variable genes (HVG) are not used for subsetting.

Related deposit (init weights, separate record): 10.5281/zenodo.20489646

This deposit provides the minimal benchmark bundle for reproducing Clifti-GPT fine-tuning, annotation, and embedding experiments. Files are split into reference (labeled training) and query (held-out test) AnnData objects per cohort, matching the layout expected under data/scgpt/benchmark/ in the Clifti-GPT codebase.

File

Cohort

Contents inside zip

ms.zip

Multiple Sclerosis

ms/reference_annot.h5ad, ms/query_annot.h5ad

hp5.zip

Human Pancreas

hp5/reference.h5ad, hp5/query.h5ad

lung.zip

Lung-Kim

lung/reference_annot.h5ad, lung/query_annot.h5ad

cl.zip

Cell line

cl/reference.h5ad, cl/query.h5ad

covid.zip

COVID-19 (uncorrected)

covid/reference-raw.h5ad, covid/query-raw.h5ad, covid/Covid.h5ad

myeloid.zip

Myeloid (base)

myeloid/reference_adata.h5ad, myeloid/query_adata.h5ad

data.title: Clifti-GPT benchmark AnnData: reference and query splits for federated single-cell experiments

metadata.title: EpiFlow Panel Builder: a spectrally aware reagent-selection tool for multiparametric flow cytometry
metadata.description:
Serrano Lab | Release of V1.2 for Zenodo Publication

Description EpiFlow Panel Builder is a single-page web application for designing spectrally aware flow cytometry panels, built around a curated inventory of 221 reagents from the Serrano Lab at Boston University's Center for Regenerative Medicine. The tool operates one step earlier than spectral viewers: it helps users select which reagents to pull from a lab inventory before instrument setup, flagging detector conflicts and spectral congestion via emission-peak heuristics. It is organized around the 11-marker Core EpiFlow panel for histone H3 post-translational-modification profiling, with layerable biology modules (Neural, Pericyte, Vascular, Cardiac, Immune, NOTCH, and others), and supports the Cytek Aurora 5L and BD FACSDiscover S8. When a panel hits a detector or fluorochrome conflict, the tool suggests same-marker alternatives on different conjugates for one-click swaps.

This release (v1.2) integrates a Round-3 clone-ID verification, raising clone coverage to 205/221 (93%), corrects conjugation and catalog-number errors against vendor product pages, unifies marker labels for the NG2 and Nestin families, and adds a spectral-compatibility disclaimer. Detector assignments are heuristics, not measured spillover; users are responsible for corroborating spectral compatibility in dedicated spectral panel-design software and with single-stained controls.

Author contributions (CRediT): S.S. —  panel design, validation, investigation, software (testing), writing (review and editing). M.A.S. — conceptualization, supervision, resources, funding acquisition, software (architecture and data curation), writing (original draft and review). 

Version v1.2.1

Language English

Keywords flow cytometry; spectral cytometry; panel design; antibody panel; fluorochrome; Cytek Aurora; BD FACSDiscover S8; immunophenotyping; reagent inventory; research software

creators.name: Sreerama, Sandeep
creators.affiliation: Center for Regenerative Medicine, Boston University

creators.name: Serrano, Angie
creators.affiliation: Center for Regenerative Medicine, Boston University

data.title: EpiFlow Panel Builder: a spectrally aware reagent-selection tool for multiparametric flow cytometry

metadata.title: AF4-UV-MALS size distribution evaluations of iron oxide nanoparticle samples (IONP) - Retention time approaches
metadata.description:
This dataset presents AF4-UV-MALS size measurements of iron oxide nanoparticle.
The nanoparticles were synthetized by BAM and measured at SMD (and also at LNE, data not included here).
The small size of the sample (~10 nm) makes the measurement by MALS (inline of AF4) very challenging: weak signal, and almost not size information. AF4 has the advantage of having a theoretical description. Elution time relates directly to hydrodynamical size (working principle of the method). This leads to other sizing methods (size distribution measurement methods, actually), based on extrapolation of external calibration (with several size standard, relating size to elution time) but also taking advantage of the theory (single parameter to calibrate: effective channel thickness) in its linear model, in isocratic mode, which admit a mathematical analytical closed form. The flow program here developed is isocratic in the beginning of the run, where the sample is fully fractionated. 

This dataset shows that size distribution measurement can be more efficient using retention time, when detector reach their limits, for example, ~sizes <20 nm (order of magnitude - sample dependent) are difficult to measure with MALS.
Application of the theory is also shown to be more versatile with respect to size standards samples and sample to be analyzed: flows can be adjusted to better suit optimal separation conditions (flow program) of each of the samples. 
On the other hand, external calibration (curve), is limited to application of exactly the same flow program.

The model here applied follows the following equation :
$$ d= 2 \frac{t_r}{w_{eff}^2} \frac{k_B T}{\pi \eta} \left(1/\ln\left(1+\frac{\dot{V}_x}{\dot{V}_d}\frac{A(z)}{A_{tot}}\right)\right)$$
that can be found in the following references :

  • 10.1016/j.chroma.2012.07.029
  • 10.1021/ac00052a025
  • 10.1021/ac00010a013

creators.name: de carsalade du pont, valentin

creators.name: Matiushkina, Anna

metadata.title: Flow Cytometry (FCS) files related to "Epistatic impacts of cis- and trans-regulatory mutations on the distribution of mutational effects for gene expression in Saccharomyces cerevisiae"
metadata.description:
The files included in this Zenodo record support “Epistatic impacts of cis- and trans-regulatory mutations on the distribution of mutational effects for gene expression in Saccharomyces cerevisiae” by Eden McQueen, Bing Yang, and Patricia J. Wittkopp, which is available on BioRxiv at https://doi.org/10.1101/2025.03.26.645465. Questions about these files can be sent to the corresponding author, Patricia J. Wittkopp, at wittkopp@umich.edu.

For additional information, see the included "Zenodo README.pdf" file.
